text: Just an Idea for a wind capturing wall. When I was a kid I loved to play with things that fly made from paper. author: @claytonmccormick622
text: the problem or opportunity depending on where you live is high wind is easy low wind is not so much windmill turns to fast it may come apart  what is the best optimisation for where you are  if it does not turn fast enough you can capture no work this is why windmills work best high up and in a place with lots of natural wind this is why i think it is best to make the blade assembly as light as possible and putting the generator and gears and such on the ground where they are easy to work on there are lots of way to do that my thought was a front yard with lots of windmills pumping water uphill with a tank to collect the pumped water and an alternator and watermill to convert all the little powers into 1 big power.

author: @stevenw4549
text: I would imagine weight is becoming an issue. Don't think it will create much electricity as it is. Have you seen the design that utilizes two curved cones that are stationary at the top one is above the other? Wind from any direction is deflected downward  through conduit it is guided to a turbine to generate electricity. The only moving part is the turbine. The cones can be higher up to catch the wind and deflected downward.

author: @time2go265
text: ive been flying rc planes for 20+ years now... i played with wind generators for a while 12 years ago......theres no easy way to explain this. think about a wind generator like the prop on a airplane. bigger prop requires more torque to spin. the bigger your foils are the more TQ you will have. weight is essential in rotating mass. i used foam board and a geared electric motor for my prototypes and card board and 1 layer of fiber glass for the 2nds. make sure you machine is balanced and CG is right. the best low wind wing i came up with was a delta wing style foil made of cardboard and 1 layer of fiber glass. if you have a square center leading edge you will grab more wind. hope this helps

author: @billunderwood3392
text: Tell me if I'm crazy. How about a combo bearing and generator? I.e., mount an upside down pie tin to the bottom of your turbine, filled with magnets in epoxy, north poles facing down. Then an opposing plate with copper coils but also with, say 3 magnets, North Pole upwards. Seems like you would end up with a nearly friction-free bearing, and every breeze would generate electricity in the coils.

author: @Cooliemasteroz
text: While this is interesting it’s hard to tell if it works well without comparing it to something that you know works well. I don’t know if you have those spinning roof vents in England but here in Australia they are a practical way to get some cooling by getting hot air out of a roof. I put some on my Mums house and installed close able ceiling vents in every room and there was so much suction created that it would hold a news paper onto a ceiling vent with very little wind. Even on a still but hot day these things will still spin if fitted to a roof as the tiled roofs that most houses here have get very hot and the rising hot air is all that’s needed to make them spin. I suspect one of these roof vents might have performed better on your outside test. If you are unfamiliar with the vent I’m talking about just google Whirlybird roof vents.

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
The blades could be tilted to increase the spin in a light breeze or to decrease it in a strong wind when it's spinning too fast. Down the track this could be automated based on RPM to increase efficiency.
Also as another viewer commented, using a twin bearing system would reduce friction thus also increasing efficiency.
Are you going to spray paint the whole thing once your done, maybe a futuristic silver or just plain white.

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: Just a thought I was having but what if you shorten the pipe that holds the fin to where the fin would touch? Wouldn't that catch the air in reflecting it into a downward and upward push which should cause it to turn faster with less wind. I think it would force the wind to the center. I bottom fins would carry the wind up to the center line and the top fins would carry the wind downward to the center line. LOL, attach some plastic spoons all the way around the center line to catch that wind. I was kind of thinking also about a spiral of rope going around the whole unit, hmmm

replies.author: @bootsowen
replies.text: My thoughts exactly michel, when the "blades" are added it behaves like a waterwheel with scoops rather than aerofoils. I thought it would rotate the other way with an aerofoil.  I am fully prepared to be shouted down, but surely 6 aerofoils mounted on a (low air resistance) frame without the shapes in the centre would have the same effect.  But then we are just getting back to the territory of those vertical windmills.  Love the videos Robert, glad you took the chuck key out of the lathe.
